questions.question: Derive the formula for the number of squares remaining in the Sierpinski Carpet after the nth step.
questions.answer: At each step every square produces 8 smaller squares, so the number of remaining squares follows the pattern Rn = 8^n.

questions.question: Explain how the number of holes increases in the Sierpinski Carpet pattern.
questions.answer: At each step every remaining square produces a hole, so the total number of holes increases cumulatively according to H(n+1) = Hn + Rn.

questions.question: Find the number of sides in the Koch Snowflake after n steps.
questions.answer: The number of sides increases by a factor of 4 at each step. Therefore the number of sides after n steps is 3 × 4^n.

questions.question: Explain why the perimeter of the Koch Snowflake grows indefinitely.
questions.answer: Each step multiplies the number of sides while decreasing their length, causing the total perimeter to increase without bound.

questions.question: Show that joining the midpoints of an equilateral triangle divides it into four identical equilateral triangles.
questions.answer: Joining the midpoints creates four congruent triangles with equal side lengths due to parallel mid-segments.

questions.question: Explain how fractals demonstrate infinite complexity using simple geometric rules.
questions.answer: Fractals use simple iterative rules that repeat infinitely, producing complex shapes with self-similar structures.

questions.question: Derive the number of faces, edges and vertices of a prism whose base is an n-sided polygon.
questions.answer: Faces = n + 2, Edges = 3n, Vertices = 2n.

questions.question: Derive the number of faces, edges and vertices of a pyramid with an n-sided base.
questions.answer: Faces = n + 1, Edges = 2n, Vertices = n + 1.

questions.question: Explain how nets help determine the shortest path on the surface of a cuboid.
questions.answer: By unfolding the cuboid into a net, the surface path becomes a straight line which represents the shortest distance.

questions.question: Explain why the shortest path between two points on a cuboid surface is not always along the edges.
questions.answer: The shortest path is obtained by unfolding the cuboid and drawing a straight line on the net.

questions.question: Explain how different unfoldings of a cuboid can produce different path lengths.
questions.answer: Different nets position the two points differently, resulting in different straight-line distances.

questions.question: Explain why projections of parallel lines remain parallel.
questions.answer: Projection preserves direction relationships, so parallel lines remain parallel in the projection.

questions.question: Explain why different objects may have identical projections.
questions.answer: Projection removes depth information, so different objects may produce identical two-dimensional projections.

questions.question: Explain how shadows approximate projections when the light source is very far away.
questions.answer: When the light source is distant, rays become parallel and the shadow becomes identical to the projection.

questions.question: Explain why projections are useful in engineering design.
questions.answer: Multiple projections help represent all dimensions of an object clearly for construction.

questions.question: Explain why the isometric projection of a cube appears as a hexagon.
questions.answer: When viewed from a corner, the edges project equally forming a hexagonal outline.

questions.question: Explain how isometric grids help represent three-dimensional shapes.
questions.answer: The grid provides equal spacing along three axes, allowing accurate drawing of 3D objects.

questions.question: Explain how Tetris-like cube arrangements help understand isometric drawings.
questions.answer: They help visualize how cubes connect in three dimensions and how edges align on isometric grids.

questions.question: Explain how impossible triangles create optical illusions.
questions.answer: Impossible triangles combine inconsistent projections that appear possible in two dimensions but cannot exist in three dimensions.

questions.question: Explain the importance of visualisation in solving geometric problems.
questions.answer: Visualisation helps understand spatial relationships and solve complex geometry problems.

questions.question: Explain how stacking cubes can produce different projections.
questions.answer: Different cube arrangements change the visible faces, producing different projections.

questions.question: Explain how nets help build three-dimensional models.
questions.answer: Nets provide a flat template that can be folded to form three-dimensional objects.

questions.question: Explain how fractals are used in computer graphics.
questions.answer: Fractals generate realistic natural patterns like mountains, trees and clouds in computer graphics.

questions.question: Explain the mathematical idea behind recursive geometric constructions.
questions.answer: Recursive constructions repeat the same geometric rule at each step to generate complex patterns.

questions.question: Explain how fractal patterns combine mathematics and art.
questions.answer: Fractals create visually appealing patterns while following strict mathematical rules.
